sentences.text: Well, of course, as I say, we were just following orders from Beamish, and from Fry, and so forth.
What I would do, quite frequent, was go and bring them in through my secret passage and all into Marseille.
Yes, and then we would try to find hiding places for them.
Some of the gangsters were quite helpful.
You had to pay them, but they were quite helpful.
And we even had one.
He was Czechoslovakian originally, but he and a Frenchmen were sort of running some sort of a racket.
I don't know what.
And when we ran out of money, which we often did, he would say, well, charge it.
We'll put it on the cuff, so to speak.
And it was quite helpful, and they were very good at hiding people because they were used to that.
And the Germans didn't like to go up in the old part because so many had disappeared up there.
The old part was something.
It was like Casbah in Algiers.
Oh, yes, Bill Fryer.
He was wonderful.
He was a little mousy-type person, and in the daytime he'd go down and draw people's portraits on the dock for money.
And then at night, he'd sit there and with a little brush he would-- he'd do the whole operation.
They'd give him a passport that we would buy from different people.
When I say 'we,' in this case it was Beamish, usually.
They'd buy these passports, and he would take a razor blade, take it off, the picture off, and put the new one on of the person he wanted.
And then with a little brush, he would draw the stamp, and then he'd take sandpaper, and a little bit of coffee, and water--
Age it and make it look authentic.
And he was brilliant at it, and he did a lot of them.
From Vienna.
He was one of the-- he was on the list.
Yes.
Oh, and how.
He was-- and trouble is with him, he looked so Jewish that there was no way on earth that he could have.
It was like Walter Mehring, you know?
Dear Little Walter Mehring, who was about came up here on me, and he was, you know, the famous poet.
And he wrote that beautiful poem, Phantom It was about a ship that some other gangsters had arranged.
They were going to take-- to Africa, going to take a big group of people.
And I was assigned to go down with them and wait there until we had to go outside of the harbor because-- you know, the harbor was always controlled.
And the big fishing boat would come outside of the harbor, pick them up, and I would come back into town, and they would go on to safety in North Africa.
Not so sure, because the Italians and the Germans were patrolling out there in the Mediterranean, too, you know, so it would have been a-- but anyway, the boat never came.
They'd been cheated.
Later on we learned to tear money in half, you know, and give half now and half later.
But then we gave half before the whole thing, half the money, and of course, we never saw the boat or the gangsters.
And Walter Mehring wrote this lovely poem called And finally it was getting daylight, and we had to leave because we couldn't be seen.
You know, the trouble, the whole thing, most of the Jews looked Jewish, and we couldn't be seen, a group of people walking back into-- into Marseille, so we had to separate.
And that was like a-- I remember very well one of them organized it like storm troopers.
You go there, you go here, you wait there, and you do this, how you get back into town.
And that's the way it happened.
About 20 some.
[LAUGHS] Not one got caught.
Fry was-- I don't know, God was with us, let me tell you, because how-- how it worked I just do not know.
As I say, I had nothing to do with the-- with the planning.
I was just running errands.
I was doing what they told me to do.
Well, no, they told us everything.
We knew everything.
Oh, yeah, we would sit in on the meetings and all.
But I didn't open my mouth, naturally, but we knew the whole thing.
Had to because, you know, you couldn't hide that.
Well, that was my most passionate thing that ever-- I'd ever dreamed of.
It was my whole life at that time.
Well, first place, seeing how they were treated.
You know, we know the thing pretty well.
We'd been pretty well instructed.
And-- and then, you know-- and then also, the fact that we knew these people were talented and that should-- and we also knew how much they were going to help America.
They did so much for America.
But that wasn't the thing.
It was just that, you know, we liked them, and we-- if they had been-- maybe if they'd been Chinese we would have felt the same, but it just happened to be that it was Jewish, you know?
And I felt this very big friendship with them because of my past friendships.
And you also had something very strong in common with your art.
Oh, and we-- I could talk to them.
I couldn't talk to a lot of my friends.
You know, the sort of ones that I'd gone over there to drive an ambulance with.
I couldn't talk to them much because we didn't have very much in common.
We could talk about football and stuff like that, but with these people, you could sit there and--
Oh.
It was-- I had a lot of good friends in all that crowd.
And Dr.
Wolf and Ellen [INAUDIBLE].
You know, the actress and all.
And we used to sit in the cafe, and I'd say, but you can't-- oh, and Mehring, this little-- you know, the poet-- if he was with Fry or me, he would go anywhere.
We couldn't do anything to protect him, but he just felt, oh, you're American.
Nothing can happen to me if I'm with you.
Something good happened to him, I can tell you.
An excitement in a way.
A little bit-- a little bit afraid all the time, and your fear was divided.
You were afraid for them.
Not so much for yourself because, first place, after all, we were Americans, and we could possibly find a way, you know, with an American passport.
It wasn't going to be nothing like as bad as it would be for them.
But there was sort of an excitement and all.
I hate the word 'dedication,' but that's way Fry was.
He was just determined that he was-- there's no way.
If he had-- if he knew that he was going to a camp the next day, he still would have stayed.
He was wonderful.
And so was Beamish, and so were Jean [INAUDIBLE].
These were wonderful people.
I was lucky to know people like that, you know?
I was lucky to know some of these Jewish because we didn't know who they were.
They were numbers to us.
A lot of them didn't even have names, you know.
We just called them 'that man over in the such-and-such a [NON-ENGLISH].' Yeah.
Did you find it difficult when you asked or offered to hide people for you and help you?
You had to go about it very discretely.
And you had to do sometimes-- sometimes you had to make two or three meetings with these people before you could really determine if they would be trustworthy.
And a good way to do it would be to let slip something that as they could be interpreted as pro-Nazi and see how they reacted.
Then you had to rush and straighten that out in a hurry.
And you make a-- Fry told us how to do this.
You know, I think Jean [INAUDIBLE] told us.
He would teach you just like a dialogue coach, you know, and drop something that could mean that you were a collaborator.
And then you'd see their reaction, then you had to quickly say, hm, good, your reaction was perfect.
And then that put them at ease then.
Then you knew that they were--
--more or less on your side of the fence.
There were A lot of tricks that you could find out, you know.
The gangsters, all of them.
But as I say, there was one-- one who we ran a big charge account with.
A big one.
Well, we knew more or less where we shouldn't go.
There were certain places you could go that were quite all right.
We knew where we shouldn't be.
We knew which bars not to hang around in.
But you had to hang out a little bit or they would have been suspicious.
You had to-- I mean, we all knew some girls, you know, naturally, and that was the best way in the world.
Whenever I would have meetings with Fry, which I did quite often, we always met in cafes.
Well, in the office, too, but finally it got so I couldn't go to the office anymore because they knew they were watching me.
But we'd meet in cafes, and I'd always take a girl with me.
And of course, the girl couldn't under-- I always made sure she didn't understand English, and it just looked like three people sitting there, you know--
At one time, when-- when [INAUDIBLE] came to town, they locked up a lot of people, and they-- they really made a big thing when he came to town.
No, you didn't.
You were very sure not to.
The few times that there was a curfew, you didn't do anything.
Only when things like when [INAUDIBLE] came to town.
Yes.
Well, I was questioned a couple of times, you know.
When you say 'questioned,' they didn't beat you, you know what I mean?

In fact, the dangerous ones when they questioned you were the nice ones, the ones who were sweet and sort of-- because they would lull you into a false sense of-- because there was one who we knew he was the worst one of all, and we warned one or two of our friends when you go there, you play right along with it.
But remember, he's worse than any of them.
Because he would be so nice, and your coffee, you know.
And then he'd turn the knife.
But boy, he sure had a-- he had a technique, let's put it that way.

Well, we were just being terribly careful about who came into this office where the refugee assistance was, being very careful and being sure that there were no passports or anything left around and the papers were there.

And whenever I would go to send, you know, this rough section where you had to go to send the telegrams, had to pay for it in cash, and that was the trouble, was protecting the cash because it was right in the section where gangsters were, and I carried it in my sock all the time.
There were-- there were things that-- you learned fast, let's put it that way.
And as I say, adrenaline is a great help.
It sort of-- when you're scared, you can do a lot of things that you can't do otherwise.
Nature did a lot for us, you know.
No.
In fact, never have.
Even today I doubt they do.
Oh, yes, by now they do because the-- the British published some things one time.
And also there have been a lot of things published since the Holocaust Museum opened.
But until that time, practically none of my friends knew anything about it.
There's a big-- it varies greatly.
Some books have said 2,000.
Others say 2,400.
The Holocaust Museum, I think, said 2,300 or something.
I would say that was pretty good.

What I wanted to emphasize was that while Fry were sent there to rescue these enemies of, you know, the people really on the list, and mostly artists, and writers, and musicians, and politicians, and all, anybody who was in danger could always be helped by him.
He would help them.
And if he couldn't help them, he would give them money.
And he had-- they found a gangster who would take the money, give money here, and then his clients would take it in America or in Switzerland or wherever, and we had ways of doing that.
That wasn't difficult.
Mrs.
Roosevelt, I think, helped in that.
So the money came in from Mrs.
Roosevelt and her group.
And as it went on and on and was getting more and more successful, then it was much easier to-- although, sometimes we ran awfully low.
We were going-- and then my money was coming from this friend of mine, Bill Holland.
He had quite a bit, and he was waiting for me to go to the RAF with him.
And he was hanging out and just-- he didn't help us very much because he looked too German, you know?
He looked-- but it was good to be seen with him because he looked German, and he was German descent.
And he had driven an ambulance with me during the war.
And then he went with me to the RAF, and he was shot down on his third flight.
And another one who was in the ambulance corps waited for us in Lisbon, and then on the ship going over, he was sunk and killed.
Oh, I guess not a year.
Not quite a year.
I don't know the dates and all.
When you get this book-- and also get the one of Mary Jane Gold.
That's a very good book-- it tells you exactly the dates and everything.
Oh, yes.
Well into '41.
summer, thank God.
So it was fairly comfortable.
And then, of course, Bill and I were in different squadrons, you know.
We were in different-- he made a better grade than I did.
I was already sick and didn't know it, and then later on, I was discharged with tuberculosis and sent back to Canada.
That's where the last time I told I saw my brother.
Yes.
As I'm-- you see, the refugees presented problems, too, sometimes.
Some-- some were scared to death and showed it.
Some you couldn't convince them that they were-- that they had to leave.
You had to-- you really had to-- you know, Marc Chagall, you can't imagine the problems he presented.
Fry had to practically drag him out.
He said no, no, I'm famous, they're not going to do anything to me.
And boy, he was way up on the list.
We had some pretty good information who was on the list, you know.
And Konrad Heiden you know, had done the best-- the best biography on Hitler.
And all these people were-- I mean, Franz Werfel, you know, he was hiding in a bordello in Lourdes when he wrote when he started it.
Yes.
Some of them were very-- you know, they had to be fairly energetic, and a number of them, for example-- I'm trying to think of some of them who were a real problem.
[INAUDIBLE] was a real problem, and there was-- well, Werfel was quite heavy, you know, and had heart trouble.
And-- and some of them were not very physically able to do it, and it was a problem getting them to-- persuading them to do it.
Very small groups, yes.
There was a place there with a cemetery, and that was the meeting point.
And then you'd go over into Spain, and then you had to come back to about 200 yards from where you had started from the-- you see, here's the French--
Border, and the customs thing.
And you go through, and 100 yards further down the railroad track is the Spanish border.
Well, they had to go around like this, and come back, and get as if they were just getting off the train, so to speak, and--
Get their stamps.
If they didn't, if they were caught in Spain without that stamp, entrance stamp, they would be in big trouble, of course.
And that was-- I mean, you imagine then--
Everybody in it did it somewhere.
I think Beamish was the one who invented that--
That route.

And then there was another man named Jonathan and Lisa Fittko who-- he had been a journalist, and he had been-- he was an expert on getting over borders because he'd been chased all over Europe by the Gestapo.
Excuse me.
Because some of the articles he had written which I don't think Mr.
Hitler was very pleased with.
And he and his wife had rented a house down near [INAUDIBLE], and they were working there to make a living in the [INAUDIBLE].
And I think-- I think it was Beamish, or Jean [INAUDIBLE], contacted them, and what they would do is people would come there and stay with him as if they were subletting a room or something.
Then they would go out into the fields on a picnic or something, and he showed them-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh, I'm so sorry.
Show them how to get across the mountains.
And it worked, let's put it that way.
A lot of people went over that way.
We had a lot of other ideas that didn't work.
Like for example, we were going to take a boat and just go around like that and come back on Portbou, get to Portbou, but never did materialize.
So many of our plans never materialized.
And obviously, some people were caught, but there was a Major [INAUDIBLE], a British military attache in Spain, and quite a few people.
Dr.
Bone ?] and a few people who in Spain, if they were caught, they would find ways to get them out.
Two or three people-- not two or three.
Maybe a dozen or more over the year were caught in Spain without the proper papers.
Here's what you had to have.

You had to have-- before the Portuguese would let you in-- we think there were a lot of collaborators in Portugal, a lot of them-- before they would let you in, you had to have a visa saying that you were going somewhere else because too many people were there, were over populating.
I mean, it was unbelievable.
You couldn't get a hotel anywhere.
I remember the Chinese visa said, under no circumstances is the bearer of this-- this visa allowed into China.
[LAUGHS] But it served the purpose because nobody read Chinese, you see.
But you had to have that visa and a passage on the ship or on-- on the clipper.
The clipper was just operating in those days.
And, oh, there were-- a lot of bribing went on.
A lot of-- gangsters were helpful, let's put it that way.
And a lot of people with ties and all with gangsters, too, I have to tell you.
And then a lot of wonderful people who did wonderful things, did dangerous things.
And this-- Harry Bingham, you know, the vice-consul.
He could have ruined his career and a lot of other things.
Career is nothing, but what could have happened to him.
There were some wonderful people in that time.
I think those moments bring out the best and the worst in people.
And Fry was the kind of man who you either laughed at him or you really loved him.
And I think sometimes these people say, oh, what could he do?
Well, he did it.
I had to make a speech, a big thing, with the opening of the museum at the-- at the Department of Commerce.
Big, bit thing.
Another one in the rotunda at the Capitol.
And I said that it should have been mission accomplished rather than surrender on demand, because surrender on demand is a little bit negative for what he did.
That's his book, the name of his book.
You must get that book.
And get Mary Jane's book.
It's good.
Danny Benedict wrote one in French, too.
A lot of people wrote about this.
[LAUGHS] No.
Not yet, no.
As I say, you know, there's not-- it's in the back of your mind, you know.

A lot of things have happened, and as I say, most of my friends-- a girl I knew quite well in Rome rushed up to me one day, and she'd read something in one of the American magazines about me, and said, I didn't know you'd ever done anything.
They were the ones who were doing it.
We were just following orders.
Well, no.
I was the, I suppose-- well, yes, I suppose.
Yes, I think.
No.
The only time, yes.
We had some things that we had to-- and he was using toothpaste and things, putting in that, and you know good and well that's not going to work very long.
Maybe once or twice.
And also a refugee who was thinking that, well, he'd get rid of it in a hurry, and some of our messages might not get through that way.
But we had a series of messages that had-- at the very end there-- that had to get through, and that's what they did.
What they did is type them on very thin paper, you know, and rolled it up very tight, and put it in--
Telling who the people were, and where they were, and how to contact them, and so on.

Yeah, they had to do-- all to do with the refugees, what was needed for them and who they were, because-- and the sort of things that, as Fry said in his book, when I was arrested in Spain, he said it produced panic in them-- panic in them because they knew how sensitive this stuff was.
But thanks to that-- those pornographic drawings, and thanks to that chasing, carrying the luggage for those people, we didn't get caught.
And as I say, adrenaline is a great--
In Lisbon, eventually.
Spain, and then all through Spain.
And of course, they had to have these Spanish-- and there was a man I got to know later.
Name is Carlos [? de Rafael. ?] He had been in the consulate there, and later on I ran into him in Spain when I was filming there.
And he remembered me, and came up, and said, oh, my God, you're the man who didn't sell us passports, gave us passports, quite a few.
Yes.
Well, then it's all right, but then the Americans were responsible for getting them out.
They had to already have their ticket, and they had to have a visa somewhere, Congo or anywhere.
But in most cases, for America, you see.
I will say this.
America-- of course, America profited a lot from it by these brilliant people, but in the long run--
When it came down to it, they did.
They weren't very helpful to us.
In fact, they were trying to get us out as fast as they could because, you know, they were-- we were rocking the boat.
And Vichy was the government at that time, and they to-- they wanted to play ball with Vichy.
